locked_facts: Phase angle φ = arctan((X_L - X_C) / R)
If X_L > X_C: circuit is inductive, V leads I
If X_C > X_L: circuit is capacitive, I leads V
If X_L = X_C: circuit is at resonance, Z = R (minimum), φ = 0
V_R, V_L, V_C cannot be added arithmetically — they are out of phase
V_L and V_C are exactly 180° out of phase with each other
truth_statements: Total voltage across LCR is NOT the arithmetic sum of V_R + V_L + V_C
Impedance Z is always greater than or equal to R
V_L and V_C subtract from each other in the phasor diagram because they are 180° apart
At resonance, V_L and V_C cancel and Z = R

aha_moment.what_to_show: Show that V_L = 150V and V_C = 100V in a circuit where V_source = 50V and V_R = 50V. Student sees that V_L > V_source. This seems impossible until the phasor diagram reveals that V_L and V_C nearly cancel (150-100=50), leaving only the R component. The source only needs to supply 50V because the L and C voltages are internally fighting each other.
aha_moment.visual_emphasis: Number the voltages explicitly: V_R = 50V, V_L = 150V, V_C = 100V, V_source = 50V. First show arithmetic sum = 300V (wrong). Then show phasor addition giving 50V (correct). The cognitive dissonance and its resolution is the aha moment.

simulation_states.label: LCR circuit — three elements in series
simulation_states.what_student_sees: Circuit with AC source, R, L, C in series. Same current I flows through all three since they are in series.
simulation_states.what_to_emphasize: Series means same current everywhere. But each element develops a voltage that may be in different phase from the current.

simulation_states.label: Individual voltages — phasors
simulation_states.what_student_sees: Phasor diagram: V_R (along current direction, 0°), V_L (90° ahead of I), V_C (90° behind I). All three phasors shown rotating together.
simulation_states.what_to_emphasize: V_R is in phase with I. V_L is 90° ahead. V_C is 90° behind. Crucially: V_L and V_C point in OPPOSITE directions in the phasor diagram.

simulation_states.label: Resultant voltage — phasor addition
simulation_states.what_student_sees: V_L and V_C combined first: net reactive voltage = V_L - V_C (since they oppose). This net reactive voltage added at 90° to V_R gives V_source. Source voltage phasor is the hypotenuse of the right triangle.
simulation_states.what_to_emphasize: The formula V = √(V_R² + (V_L - V_C)²) comes directly from this right triangle. It is Pythagoras applied to phasors.

simulation_states.label: Effect of frequency on circuit character
simulation_states.what_student_sees: Frequency slider. At low f: X_C >> X_L, circuit is capacitive. At high f: X_L >> X_C, circuit is inductive. At resonant f₀: X_L = X_C, circuit is purely resistive, Z is minimum.
simulation_states.what_to_emphasize: The character of the circuit (inductive vs capacitive) depends on frequency. The crossover point where X_L = X_C is resonance.

common_misconceptions.student_belief: The source voltage = V_R + V_L + V_C added arithmetically
common_misconceptions.actual_physics: V_R, V_L, V_C are out of phase. They must be added as phasors. V_source = √(V_R² + (V_L - V_C)²). Arithmetic sum will always give a wrong (too large) answer.
common_misconceptions.simulation_emphasis: Show a numerical example where arithmetic sum = 300V but phasor sum = 50V. Make the student compute both. The discrepancy forces recognition that phase matters.

trigger_keywords: voltage across inductor greater than source
how can V_L be more than V
common_misconceptions.student_belief: It is impossible for the voltage across L or C to exceed the source voltage
common_misconceptions.actual_physics: In an LCR circuit, V_L and V_C can both individually exceed the source voltage. This is because they are 180° apart and mostly cancel each other in the phasor diagram — only their difference appears in the source.
common_misconceptions.simulation_emphasis: Show specific numbers: V_source = 100V, V_L = 300V, V_C = 250V. Phasor diagram shows V_L - V_C = 50V reactive, V_R = 86.6V, giving √(86.6² + 50²) = 100V = V_source. Make the cancellation visible.
